directions.name: Keep the peeled steady state, but restore the proven final-two-tile handoff to recover correctness
directions.hypothesis: Round 4 produced the clearest positive runtime signal in many rounds: the dominant 256x128 hot-band kernel dropped from 41.080 ms to 40.365 ms and tensor activity rose from 38.75% to 39.28%. That means the fixed-shape steady-state peeling direction is probably correct, but the manual epilogue around the last two K-tiles is not. Instead of abandoning the direction, the next move should keep the branch-free steady-state core and hand the last two iterations back to the original, already-correct next/future/wait0 control sequence. This preserves almost all of the control-flow win while removing the highest-risk cp.async boundary change.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Correctness break in the final cp.async stage handoff of the peeled hot-band schedule, not a failure of the hot-band steady-state peeling idea itself.
directions.risk: Moderate but targeted. The performance gain is already measured, and the likely fix surface is the last-two-tile control handoff rather than a full rollback.
directions.stop_condition: Reject if correctness still fails after restoring the original tail handoff or if the repaired version gives back almost all of the 0.7 ms main-kernel win.

directions.name: Return to the accepted-correct surface and push warp-local Ps2r plus right-left register reuse
directions.hypothesis: If the peeled steady-state cannot be repaired cleanly, the next human-idea branch with real upside is still warp-local operand scheduling. On the accepted-correct surface, barrier and mio throttle were already low while tensor activity stayed below 39%, which points to warp-level operand delivery more than CTA staging. The current PTX microkernel already keeps one future B fragment live; the next step is to try a tighter Ps2r plan and an explicit right-left register reuse order inside the 64x64 warp microkernel instead of reopening CTA repacking.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Per-warp operand delivery and short-scoreboard pressure inside the 64x64 PTX hot-band microkernel.
directions.risk: Moderate to high. This branch can easily increase live fragments and push the main kernel off the current 167-register surface.
directions.stop_condition: Reject if register pressure rises materially or if short-scoreboard improves without a matching drop in hot-band kernel time.

directions.name: Try a lighter L2-friendly logical CTA swizzle over the hot-band grid
directions.hypothesis: The user's cache-locality idea is still worth one controlled revisit, but only after the grouped traversal regression was understood. The hot-band remains a 60x25 grid over the same B panel, L2 throughput is still only about 19-20%, and the PTX family now exposes one common logical hot-band kernel. A lighter logical CTA remap that preserves the tile math but groups neighboring B tiles more cache-friendly may offer a small extra gain if the scheduler issues nearby blocks together.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Inter-CTA L2 locality across the hot-band grid rather than per-warp compute scheduling.
directions.risk: Moderate. A heavier grouped CTA traversal already regressed, so this should stay minimal and reversible.
directions.stop_condition: Reject if L2 throughput stays flat or if runtime regresses like the earlier grouped-traversal attempt.

notes: Round 5/50 starts from an incorrect but materially faster steady-state peel: the main 256x128 hot-band kernel improved by roughly 0.7 ms and tensor activity rose, but all correctness cases failed. That makes dir_01 the clear recommendation: preserve the peeled steady-state core and repair correctness by restoring the original final-two-tile handoff. Dir_02 carries the warp-local Ps2r/register-reuse human-idea branch if the peel cannot be repaired cleanly, and dir_03 keeps the lighter L2-friendly CTA swizzle as the cache-locality branch.
